content:
First, observe that if any digit of \(n\) is zero then the left‐hand side (the product of the digits) is zero, so we would need
\[
n^2-15n-27=0,
\]
but that quadratic has no integer roots.  Hence none of the digits can be zero, and the product of the digits is positive.  Thus
\[
n^2-15n-27>0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad n> \frac{15+\sqrt{333}}2\approx16.62,
\]
so \(n\ge17\).  On the other hand for large \(n\) the quadratic \(n^2-15n-27\) grows much faster than the product of digits (which for a \(k\)-digit number is at most \(9^k\)), so one only needs to check the two‐digit range \(17\le n\le36\).  A quick table:

n   | digits | product P |  n²−15n−27
----|--------|-----------|------------
17  | 1,7    | 7         | 289−255−27=7  ✔  
18  | 1,8    | 8         | 324−270−27=27  
19  | 1,9    | 9         | 361−285−27=49  
⋮   | ⋮      | ⋮         | ⋮  
36  | 3,6    | 18        |1296−540−27=729  

One finds that the only solution in positive integers is \(n=17\).  Hence the sum of all such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
